perturbation.original_text: Report to the other Party, in writing, any use and/or disclosure of the Confidential Information that is not permitted or required by this Agreement of which a Party becomes aware within five (5) days of a Party=s discovery of such unauthorized use and/or disclosure
perturbation.changed_text: Report to the other Party, in writing, any use and/or disclosure of the Confidential Information that is not permitted or required by this Agreement of which a Party becomes aware within thirty (30) days of a Party=s discovery of such unauthorized use and/or disclosure
perturbation.explanation: Changing the reporting timeframe for unauthorized disclosures from five (5) days to thirty (30) days could be problematic under HIPAA if the agreement involves protected health information (PHI). HIPAA requires reporting certain breaches of PHI within a shorter timeframe.
perturbation.law_explanation: 45 CFR § 164.410 outlines the requirements for breach notification following the discovery of a breach of unsecured protected health information. While it doesn't specify a strict deadline, undue delay in reporting breaches is viewed as non-compliant. A 30-day reporting period may be deemed excessive and unreasonable, particularly for larger breaches, potentially conflicting with the intent of timely notification to affected individuals and the Department of Health and Human Services.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Federal Register :: Request Access Request Access Request Access Due to aggressive automated scraping of FederalRegister.gov and eCFR.gov, programmatic access to these sites is limited to access to our extensive developer APIs. If you are human user receiving this message, we can add your IP address to a set of IPs that can access FederalRegister.gov & eCFR.gov; complete the CAPTCHA (bot test) below and click "Request Access". This process will be necessary for each IP address you wish to access the site from, requests are valid for approximately one quarter (three months) after which the process may need to be repeated. Request Access for 72.74.68.69 An official website of the United States government. If you want to request a wider IP range, first request access for your current IP, and then use the "Site Feedback" button found in the lower left-hand side to make the request.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Breach Notification Rule | HHS.gov Skip to main content Official websites use .gov A .gov website belongs to an official government organization in the United States. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock A locked padlock ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Navigate to: Breach Notification Rule The HIPAA Breach Notification Rule, 45 CFR §§ 164.400-414, requires HIPAA covered entities and their business associates to provide notification following a breach of unsecured protected health information. Similar breach notification provisions implemented and enforced by the Federal Trade Commission (FTC) , apply to vendors of personal health records and their third party service providers, pursuant to section 13407 of the HITECH Act. Definition of Breach A breach is, generally, an impermissible use or disclosure under the Privacy Rule that compromises the security or privacy of the protected health information. An impermissible use or disclosure of protected health information is presumed to be a breach unless the covered entity or business associate, as applicable, demonstrates that there is a low probability that the protected health information has been compromised based on a risk assessment of at least the following factors: The nature and extent of the protected health information involved, including the types of identifiers and the likelihood of re-identification; The unauthorized person who used the protected health information or to whom the disclosure was made; Whether the protected health information was actually acquired or viewed; and The extent to which the risk to the protected health information has been mitigated. Covered entities and business associates, where applicable, have discretion to provide the required breach notifications following an impermissible use or disclosure without performing a risk assessment to determine the probability that the protected health information has been compromised. There are three exceptions to the definition of “breach.” The first exception applies to the unintentional acquisition, access, or use of protected health information by a workforce member or person acting under the authority of a covered entity or business associate, if such acquisition, access, or use was made in good faith and within the scope of authority. The second exception applies
perturbation.justification: The modified text extends the reporting timeframe for unauthorized disclosures of confidential information from five to thirty days. This creates a contradiction with HIPAA regulations, which require timely breach notification. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both highlight concerns that a 30-day reporting period may be deemed excessive, especially for larger breaches. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing the HIPAA Breach Notification Rule (45 CFR §§ 164.400-414), states that covered entities must provide notification following a breach of unsecured protected health information, suggesting an expectation of prompt action. While the rule doesn't specify an exact deadline, the changed text's extended timeframe weakens the legal obligation for timely reporting, creating an inconsistency and a potential violation.

perturbation.original_text: Return to a Party or destroy, as requested by a Party, within fifteen (15) days of the termination of this Agreement, any Confidential Information provided to such Party and in its possession and retain no copies or back-up tapes
perturbation.changed_text: Return to a Party or destroy, as requested by a Party, within ninety (90) days of the termination of this Agreement, any Confidential Information provided to such Party and in its possession and retain no copies or back-up tapes
perturbation.explanation: Extending the deadline for returning or destroying confidential information to 90 days after termination could be considered unreasonable and could potentially violate state trade secret laws which require reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. A longer timeframe increases the risk of unauthorized disclosure or misuse.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute § 688.003(1) requires taking reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. Delaying the return or destruction of confidential information for 90 days after the termination of the agreement, especially when a shorter period would suffice, may be viewed as a failure to take reasonable steps to prevent misappropriation, particularly if the information constitutes a trade secret.

Home > Laws > 2023 Florida Statutes > Title XXXIX > Chapter 688 > Section 003 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2023 Florida Statutes (Including 2023C) Title XXXIX COMMERCIAL RELATIONS Chapter 688 UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT Entire Chapter SECTION 003 Injunctive relief. F.S. 688.003 688.003 Injunctive relief. — (1) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (2) In exceptional circumstances, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. Exceptional circumstances include, but are not limited to, a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. (3) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. History. — s. 3, ch. 88-254.
perturbation.justification: The modified text extends the deadline for returning confidential information to 90 days, which contradicts Florida Statute § 688.003(1) that requires reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. The law snippet states, "(1) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined." Extending the return deadline increases the risk of misappropriation, potentially violating the requirement to take reasonable measures to prevent such an event. The original 15-day period appears more aligned with 'reasonable measures' than the extended 90-day period. Thus a legal contradiction is created because the modified text may fail to take reasonable steps to prevent misappropriation.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall become effective on the Effective Date and shall continue in effect until either Party informs the other Party with thirty (30) day prior written notice of termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall become effective on the Effective Date and shall continue in effect until either Party informs the other Party with seven (7) day prior written notice of termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the termination notice period to seven (7) days may be problematic if the agreement is interpreted as a franchise agreement under Florida law. Florida law requires longer notice periods for franchise terminations.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute § 817.416 regulates franchise agreements and addresses fraudulent practices. If this agreement is deemed to be a franchise agreement a seven day termination period might violate the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. While it doesn't explicitly state 30 days it does require a reasonable time for franchisees to wind down operations or address the reasons for termination which seven days may not be. Furthermore, depending on the specific circumstances, a sudden 7-day termination could be seen as a fraudulent or unfair practice, particularly if the business affiliate has made significant investments based on the expectation of a longer-term relationship.

Home > Laws > 2023 Florida Statutes > Title XLVI > Chapter 817 > Section 416 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2023 Florida Statutes (Including 2023C) Title XLVI CRIMES Chapter 817 FRAUDULENT PRACTICES Entire Chapter SECTION 416 Franchises and distributorships; misrepresentations. F.S. 817.416 817.416 Franchises and distributorships; misrepresentations. — (1) DEFINITIONS. — For the purpose of this section: (a) The term “person” means an individual, partnership, corporation, association, or other entity doing business in Florida. (b) The term “franchise or distributorship” means a contract or agreement, either expressed or implied, whether oral or written, between two or more persons: 1. Wherein a commercial relationship of definite duration or continuing indefinite duration is involved; 2. Wherein one party, hereinafter called the “franchisee,” is granted the right to offer, sell, and distribute goods or services manufactured, processed, distributed or, in the case of services, organized and directed by another party; 3. Wherein the franchisee as an independent business constitutes a component of franchisor’s distribution system; and 4. Wherein the operation of the franchisee’s business franchise is substantially reliant on franchisors for the basic supply of goods. (c) The term “goods” means any article or thing without limitation, or any part of such article or thing, including any article or thing used or consumed by a franchisee in rendering a service established, organized, directed, or approved by a franchisor. (2) DECLARATIONS. — (a) It is unlawful, when selling or establishing a franchise or distributorship, for any person: 1. Intentionally to misrepresent the prospects or chances for success of a proposed or existing franchise or distributorship; 2. Intentionally to misrepresent, by failure to disclose or otherwise, the known required total investment for such franchise or distributorship; or 3. Intentionally to misrepresent or fail to disclose efforts to sell or establish more franchises or distributorships than is reasonable to expect the market or market area for the particular franchise or distributorship to sustain. (b) The execution or carrying out of a scheme, plan, or corporate organization which violates any of the provisions of this se
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the termination notice period from 30 days to 7 days, which could contradict Florida law if the agreement is deemed a franchise agreement. According to the Law Explanation, a 7-day termination period may not provide a reasonable time for franchisees to wind down operations and could be seen as an unfair practice. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines a franchise or distributorship in Florida Statute § 817.416(1)(b) and prohibits misrepresentations when selling or establishing one, as stated in § 817.416(2)(a). While the snippet doesn't specify a minimum notice period, the Law Explanation argues that a short notice period could violate the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, potentially constituting a misrepresentation or unfair practice under this statute, if significant investments were made based on a longer term expectation. Therefore, the reduction in notice creates a potential legal contradiction.
